page.extract:
Duke Ellington Meets Coleman Hawkins is a jazz album by Duke Ellington and Coleman Hawkins that was recorded on August 18, 1962, and released in February 1963 by Impulse! Records.
In 1995, The New York Times called it "one of the great Ellington albums, one of the great Hawkins albums and one of the great albums of the 1960s."


== Track listing ==
All tracks composed by Duke Ellington, except where noted.

"Limbo Jazz" – 5:14
"Mood Indigo" (Duke Ellington, Barney Bigard) – 5:56
"Ray Charles' Place" – 4:04

"You Dirty Dog" – 4:19

"The Ricitic" – 5:51
"Solitude" (Duke Ellington, Eddie DeLange) (1995 CD bonus track)  – 5:51

The keyTracks assert tracks that are not on this album.

Quartet personnel claim (Bob Berg, Sam Jones, Billy Higgins) is unverifiable - no personnel in evidence; background memory suggests George Coleman, not Berg, played tenor on this 1975 date (Berg joined for Eastern Rebellion 2) - verify externally before repair. 'Mode for Joe' and '5/4 Thing' matched.

ours.title: Eastern Rebellion
ours.label: Timeless
ours.albumDNA: Pianist Cedar Walton leads a powerhouse quartet with Bob Berg, Sam Jones, and Billy Higgins through a program of his finest compositions. Walton's sophisticated harmonic sense and effortless swing make this one of the greatest straight-ahead jazz piano albums of the 1970s. Proved that hard bop was alive and well during the fusion era. Walton's compositional brilliance—heard in classics like 'Naima's Love Song'—established him as one of jazz's most important composer-pianists.

page.extract:
Fontainebleau is a 1956 album by jazz musician Tadd Dameron. The title track, inspired by a trip to the French palace of the same name, is a through-composed composition with no solos, while "Flossie Lou" is a contrafact of "Jeepers Creepers".


== Reception ==

In a review for AllMusic, Scott Yanow wrote: "As is usual with most Dameron dates, the emphasis is on his inventive arrangements although there is space... for individual solos. Recommended."
Marc Myers of JazzWax called Fontainebleau "one of the prettiest octet albums of the 1950s," and commented: "The players on the album come together well, as if carefully selected for their tones... For me, the album is as perfect as a panoramic landscape painting... The music is delicate and cohesive, and the solos celebrate the vistas that dazzle the eye."


== Track listing ==
All tracks composed by Tadd Dameron.

"Fontainebleau" – 4:48
"Delirium" –  5:00
"The Scene Is Clean" – 5:00
"Flossie Lou" – 4:50
"Bula-Beige" – 11:20
Recorded March 9, 1956, at Van Gelder Studio, Hackensack, New Jersey.


== Personnel ==
Kenny Dorham – trumpet
Henry Coker  – trombone
Cecil Payne - baritone saxophone
Sahib Shihab – alto saxophone
Joe Alexander – tenor saxophone
Tadd Dameron – piano
John Simmons – bass
Shadow Wilson – drums


== References ==

page.shortDescription: 1965 studio album by Art Blakey and the Jazz Messengers
page.extract:
Free for All is a studio album by Art Blakey and the Jazz Messengers, recorded on February 10, 1964, and released on Blue Note Records in July 1965. It was originally titled Free Fall.
The Allmusic review by Al Campbell awards the album 4 stars and states, "This edition of the Jazz Messengers had been together since 1961 with a lineup that would be hard to beat: Freddie Hubbard on trumpet... Wayne Shorter on tenor sax, Curtis Fuller on trombone, Cedar Walton on piano, and Reggie Workman on bass. Shorter's title track is one of the finest moments in the Jazz Messengers' history."


== Composition ==
Freddie Hubbard's composition "The Core" is dedicated to the Congress of Racial Equality (CORE) and expresses "Hubbard's admiration of that organization's persistence and resourcefulness in its work for total, meaningful equality." He explained: "They're getting at the core, at the center of the kinds of change that have to take place before this society is really open to everyone. And more than any other group, CORE is getting to youth, and that's where the center of change is." Hubbard also felt that the band "got at some of the core of jazz – the basic feelings and rhythms that are at the foundation of music" on the track.
Clare Fischer's "Pensativa" was arranged by Hubbard for the occasion: "I was playing a gig in Long Island and the pianist started playing it. The mood got me, this feeling of a pensive woman. And the melody was so beautiful that, after I'd gotten home, I couldn't get it out of my mind."
The album was intended to have featured Wayne Shorter's composition "Eva" and two tracks with vocals by Wellington Blakey, bandleader Art Blakey's cousin. These were attempted, but no valid takes were recorded. Additionally, the musicians tried a second take of "Free for All", an attempt that producer Alfred Lion had to stop because Blakey's drums broke. This alternate take, first released on the limited 2014 Japanese SHM-CD remaster of the album, is three minutes shorter.


== Track listing ==


=== Original vinyl ===


=== 2014 Blue Note SHM-CD Remaster Edition (Japan Release) ===


== Personnel ==
Art Blakey – drums
Freddie Hubbard – trumpet
Curtis Fuller – trombone
Wayne Shorter – tenor saxophone
Cedar Walton – piano
Reggie Workman – double bass

page.extract:
Full House is an album by the jazz guitarist Wes Montgomery, released in 1962.


== History ==
The performance was recorded live at Tsubo in Berkeley, California, on June 25, 1962.  The session featured a quintet that included Wynton Kelly on piano, Johnny Griffin on tenor saxophone, Paul Chambers on bass, and Jimmy Cobb on drums.
The album was released on the Riverside Records label. A 1987 CD reissue in the Original Jazz Classics series was followed by a later reissue in 2007 featuring additional bonus tracks.


== Track listing ==


=== Original issue ===

== References ==

candidates.extractHead:
Full House is an album by the jazz guitarist Wes Montgomery, released in 1962.


== History ==
The performance was recorded live at Tsubo in Berkeley, California, on June 25, 1962.  The session featured a quintet that included Wynton Kelly on piano, Johnny Griffin on tenor saxophone, Paul Chambers on bass, and Jimmy Cobb on drums.
The album was released on the Riverside Records label. A 1987 CD reissue in the Original Jazz Classics series was followed by a later reissue in 2007 featuring additio

specificBadClaim: Pianist Count Basie's post-bop sensibility is on display in this 1986 session
contradictingQuote: Kansas City 8: Get Together is a 1979 studio album by Count Basie.
skepticDecision: The Wikipedia page shares the exact tracklist (same album, confirmed entity) and states it is a 1979 studio album, directly contradicting the prose's '1986 session'. 1986 is at most a reissue year.

originalWrongClaims.ourClaim: Pianist Count Basie's post-bop sensibility is on display in this 1986 session
originalWrongClaims.problem: The album is a 1979 studio album per Wikipedia (which has the identical tracklist), so framing it as 'this 1986 session' (i.e. recorded 1986) is wrong; 1986 is at most a reissue year (the MB release here is a Pablo CD, cat CD 2310-924, with an empty release date).
originalWrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Kansas City 8: Get Together is a 1979 studio album by Count Basie.

originalNotes: Right entity confirmed: MB embedded_mbid (releaseGroup 'Get Together', artist Count Basie, label Pablo) and the Wikipedia 'Kansas City 8: Get Together' page share the exact tracklist; all 5 keyTracks match. Year conflict between sources: MB releaseGroup firstReleaseDate is 1986 but its release date is empty and it is a CD reissue (CD catalog number); Wikipedia plainly states '1979 studio album'. The DNA's '1986 session' is contradicted by the 1979 recording date. Repair note: 1986 is the Pablo CD reissue year; the original Kansas City 8 album is 1979. Our short title 'Get Together' = the page's 'Kansas City 8: Get Together' (same album), so wikiLink is correct. The octet/'small-group'/'spare economical style' descriptions are editorial and consistent.

ours.title: Get Together
ours.label: Pablo
ours.albumDNA: Kansas City 8: Get Together is a small-group studio album by Count Basie, released on Pablo Records. Paring down from his famous big band, Basie leads an octet through a relaxed program of swing-rooted originals including "Ode to Pres" and "Basie's Bag," capturing the pianist's spare, economical style in an intimate setting. Pianist Count Basie's post-bop sensibility is on display in this 1986 session, a document of the contemporary era.

release.title: Count Basie Get Together
tracks: Pretty Time: I Can't Get Started / What Will I Tell My Heart / Talk of the Town / I Can't Give You Anything but Love / I'm Confessin' That I Love You

page.extract:
Kansas City 8: Get Together is a 1979 studio album by Count Basie.


== Track listing ==
"Ode to Pres" (Clark Terry) – 4:07
"Basie's Bag" (Count Basie, Ernie Wilkins) – 9:45
"Swinging on the Cusp" (Terry) – 5:41
"Like It Used to Be" (Basie) – 8:29
"My Main Men" (Basie) – 6:10
"Pretty Time: "I Can't Get Started"/"What Will I Tell My Heart"/"Talk of the Town"/"I Can't Give You Anything But Love"/"I'm Confessin' (That I Love You)" (Ira Gershwin, Vernon Duke)/(Irving Gordon, Jack Lawrence, Peter Tinturin)/(Jimmy McHugh, Dorothy Fields)(Doc Daugherty, Al J. Neiburg, Ellis Reynolds) – 9:35


== Personnel ==
Count Basie - piano
Budd Johnson - baritone saxophone, tenor saxophone
Eddie "Lockjaw" Davis - tenor saxophone
Clark Terry - trumpet, flugelhorn
Harry "Sweets" Edison - trumpet
Freddie Green - guitar
John Clayton - double bass
Gus Johnson - drums


== References ==

specificBadClaim: Recorded in 2008, Hi De Ho Man
skepticDecision: All six keyTracks match the matched release's tracklist, so it is unambiguously the same release; both the release and release-group are dated 1974. A 1974 Columbia compilation cannot be 'Recorded in 2008', and no 2008 release appears anywhere in the evidence -> factual_error on the year.

originalWrongClaims.ourClaim: Recorded in 2008, Hi De Ho Man showcases Cab Calloway
originalWrongClaims.problem: Our year 2008 contradicts the matched release date of 1974 (a Columbia compilation); no later 2008 release is shown in evidence

originalNotes: Same entity (Cab Calloway, Hi De Ho Man, Columbia compilation). Year field is wrong: MB release and release-group both date 1974, ours says 2008. keyTracks all match the MB tracklist (ours 'Fifteen Minutes Itermission' is a typo for MB 'Fifteen Minutes Intermission'; 'Jumpin Jive' and 'A Chicken Ain't Nothing but a Bird' match). Label placeholder 'Various' (MB: Columbia).

ours.title: Hi De Ho Man
ours.label: Various
ours.albumDNA: Recorded in 2008, Hi De Ho Man showcases Cab Calloway at the height of their creative powers, offering one of the finest expressions of Swing. Hi De Ho Man remains a landmark of Swing, cementing Cab Calloway's reputation as one of the defining voices of the era and earning consistent praise from critics worldwide.
